restaurant.label: Ordering Food
restaurant.tone_guidance: Warm and familiar. You're a regular here — you know what's good, what to avoid, and what the waiter's name probably is.
restaurant.cultural_guardrails: Tipping customs vary wildly — know the local norm before advising. Pointing at the menu is always okay. Don't apologize for dietary restrictions — just state them clearly.
restaurant.debrief_focus: ordering confidence, pronunciation of dish names, handling unexpected questions from staff

directions.label: Asking Directions
directions.tone_guidance: Quick and to the point. People on the street don't want a lesson — they want to help and move on. Give the user the one phrase that gets them moving in the right direction.
directions.cultural_guardrails: In some cultures, locals will give directions even when they don't know, rather than say 'I don't know.' Always confirm by asking a second person. Eye contact and a smile go a long way.
directions.debrief_focus: asking confidently, understanding the response, confirming understanding

market.label: Shopping at a Market
market.tone_guidance: Relaxed and playful. Market vendors are used to tourists and often enjoy the banter. A little humor goes a long way. Never sound desperate — that raises the price.
market.cultural_guardrails: In fixed-price markets, haggling is rude. In open markets, it's expected. Always smile when negotiating. Walking away is a tactic, not an insult. Never lowball — offer 60-70% of asking, not 20%.
market.debrief_focus: negotiation phrases, numbers and prices, reading vendor energy

hotel.label: Hotel Check-In
hotel.tone_guidance: Professional but not stiff. Hotel front desk staff are trained to be helpful — you just need to communicate clearly. No need for elaborate sentences. Short, clear requests work best.
hotel.cultural_guardrails: Always have your passport or ID ready before approaching the desk. In many countries, a passport is legally required at check-in. Tipping porters varies by country — know the local rate.
hotel.debrief_focus: handling unexpected issues (wrong room, no reservation), polite requests, understanding what you're being asked
auto_suggestions: Can I get a wake-up call?

social.label: Meeting Someone New
social.tone_guidance: Genuine interest beats perfect grammar every time. Locals love when foreigners make the effort. Coach the user to listen more than they speak — and to ask follow-up questions, not just answer them.
social.cultural_guardrails: Asking someone's age, marital status, or salary is normal in many cultures but invasive in others. Know the difference. Physical greetings (hugs, cheek kisses, bowing) vary enormously — mirror what the other person initiates.
social.debrief_focus: small talk fluency, cultural missteps to avoid, memorable phrases that made an impression
auto_suggestions: What do I say after hello?

government.label: Visa / Government Office
government.tone_guidance: Calm and formal. These interactions require precision — ambiguity can cost hours. Help the user ask clear, specific questions. Never guess at legal terms — say 'I don't understand, could you explain?'
government.cultural_guardrails: Maintain respectful formality. Do not attempt humor. Have all documents organized before approaching. If asked to wait, wait quietly. In some countries, small bribes are expected — know local norms.
government.debrief_focus: formal request phrases, handling confusion politely, understanding what documents are needed

transit.label: Public Transport
transit.tone_guidance: Fast and functional. Transit situations are time-sensitive. Give the user short, urgent phrases. Help them scan for visual cues (signs, maps) before asking verbally.
transit.cultural_guardrails: Queue etiquette is strict in some cultures, nonexistent in others. Priority seating is serious — give it up without being asked. Eating and talking on the phone varies by city.
transit.debrief_focus: asking for help on moving transit, reading station signs, understanding what you're being told

nightlife.label: Bars & Nightlife
nightlife.tone_guidance: Loose and fun. Help the user sound like they belong, not like they just arrived. Volume is up, subtlety is down. Short, punchy phrases win.
nightlife.cultural_guardrails: Know what 'cheers' means locally — getting it wrong is a faux pas in some cultures. Drink-buying customs vary. Know how to say 'no thank you' without being rude in loud environments.
nightlife.debrief_focus: social confidence, casual slang that landed well, how to read the room

hospital.label: Medical / Emergency
hospital.tone_guidance: Steady and clear. In medical situations, precise communication can matter. Help the user describe what's happening without panic. Short sentences. Point when needed.
hospital.cultural_guardrails: Know emergency numbers before you need them — they vary by country. Always carry your health insurance info. In some countries, payment upfront is required even in emergencies.
hospital.debrief_focus: clarity of symptom descriptions, emergency numbers, phrases that got the fastest help

office.label: Professional / Work
office.tone_guidance: Polished but not robotic. Professional contexts reward clarity and brevity. Help the user say exactly what they mean, without over-explaining.
office.cultural_guardrails: Business card exchange rituals vary enormously (Japan vs USA vs Middle East). Directness is valued in some cultures, indirect communication in others. Getting it wrong signals cultural ignorance.
office.debrief_focus: formality register, professional vocabulary, how to handle unexpected questions

school.label: School
school.tone_guidance: Patient and clear. These situations often involve anxious parents or confused students. Reduce pressure. Help them ask smart questions and understand what they're being told.
school.cultural_guardrails: Teacher-parent relationships vary culturally — in some systems teachers are deeply respected authority figures, in others they're service providers. Match the register of the person you're talking to.
school.debrief_focus: understanding school procedures, parent-teacher communication, forms and documents

customs.label: Airport Customs
customs.tone_guidance: Steady and clear. Immigration officers ask formulaic questions — help the user learn the expected answers, not improvise. Short sentences. Eye contact. No jokes.
customs.cultural_guardrails: Never joke with immigration officers. Answer only what is asked. Have all documents ready — passport, return ticket, accommodation address. In some countries, overstaying or wrong visa category is serious.
customs.debrief_focus: answering questions calmly, knowing your rights, standard phrases for common questions

pharmacy.label: Pharmacy
pharmacy.tone_guidance: Clear and specific. Help the user describe symptoms precisely so they get the right medication. Teach them to ask about dosage and interactions. Don't underestimate this — getting the wrong medication has consequences.
pharmacy.cultural_guardrails: In some countries, pharmacists can prescribe and are the first point of care. In others, prescription drugs require a doctor's note. Know what's OTC vs prescription locally.
pharmacy.debrief_focus: symptom description, asking for alternatives, understanding dosage instructions

emergency.label: Emergency
emergency.tone_guidance: Fast and clear. In emergencies, a single phrase said clearly is worth more than a paragraph. Give the user the 3-4 most critical phrases and make sure they can say them under stress. Calm tone — panic makes communication worse.
emergency.cultural_guardrails: Emergency numbers vary by country (not always 911). Know the local police number, ambulance number, and your country's embassy number before you need them. In some countries, reporting theft requires going to a police station in person.
emergency.debrief_focus: key emergency phrases, staying calm, what information to give first

landlord.label: Renting / Landlord
landlord.tone_guidance: Confident but respectful. Rental conversations require the user to advocate for themselves without being aggressive. Help them ask about terms clearly, push back on unfair conditions politely, and document agreements.
landlord.cultural_guardrails: Tenant rights vary enormously. In some markets, verbal agreements are binding; in others, nothing matters without paper. Know if deposits are capped by law. Photographing everything at move-in is universal good practice.
landlord.debrief_focus: negotiating terms, understanding what you're signing, asking about deposit return
auto_suggestions: I'd like to end the lease

bank.label: Bank / Money
bank.tone_guidance: Clear and thorough. Banking involves legal forms and numbers — precision matters. Help the user ask specific questions about fees and requirements before committing to anything. Teach them to ask for clarification without embarrassment.
bank.cultural_guardrails: Some banks require proof of address, employment, or residency visa to open accounts. ATM fees for foreign cards can be high — know the bank's policy. In some countries, cash is still king for everyday transactions.
bank.debrief_focus: opening an account, understanding fees, transferring money internationally

taxi.label: Taxi / Ride
taxi.tone_guidance: Street-smart and direct. Teach the user to confirm the price before getting in, insist on the meter where applicable, and know when to walk away. Confidence prevents overcharging. But don't be rude — most drivers are honest.
taxi.cultural_guardrails: Not all taxis use meters. Always agree on a price before getting in. Rideshare apps reduce scams significantly. In some cities, licensed taxis only operate from official stands — picking up from the street can mean unregulated drivers.
taxi.debrief_focus: negotiating fare, giving directions, avoiding common tourist scams

temple.label: Temple / Cultural Site
temple.tone_guidance: Respectful and curious. Help the user engage with sacred spaces without accidentally offending. Teach them a phrase of greeting or respect in the local religious tradition. The goal isn't tourism — it's genuine respect.
temple.cultural_guardrails: Always remove shoes when asked. Cover shoulders and knees — keep a scarf or sarong handy. Don't photograph people praying without permission. Don't walk into restricted areas. Silence during ceremonies is universal.
temple.debrief_focus: entering respectfully, asking appropriate questions, engaging with locals at the site

street_food.label: Street Food
street_food.tone_guidance: Fun and fast. Street food vendors are used to tourists and often love enthusiastic customers. Pointing is totally fine. A little enthusiasm goes a long way. Help the user try things they can't pronounce — that's the whole point.
street_food.cultural_guardrails: Watch how locals order — sometimes it's point-and-gesture, sometimes a full sentence. Dietary restrictions can be hard to communicate precisely — learn the words for your restrictions before you're hungry. Cash only at most stalls.
street_food.debrief_focus: ordering confidently, asking about ingredients, expressing enthusiasm in the local language

date.label: Meeting Someone
vocabulary_focus: I'd like to see you again
date.tone_guidance: Light and genuine. Help the user flirt in the local style — every culture has different norms around directness, compliments, and humor. Teach them how locals express interest without being creepy. A well-timed phrase in the local language is genuinely charming.
date.cultural_guardrails: Directness in romance varies enormously. In some cultures, bold compliments are normal and expected; in others, subtlety is key. Always read the situation. 'No' always means no, everywhere.
date.debrief_focus: natural conversation starters, local-style compliments, expressing interest without awkwardness
auto_suggestions: How do I say a compliment?
